videos.title: Chilled Cafe Ride and Boltby Bank!
videos.description:
Episode 18 - Join me on a chilled Sunday social cafe ride, then I tackle some more brutal Yorkshire hills, including Boltby Bank. One tough hill, made even harder by the hilly approach and tricky surface on the climb in parts, as you will see! Worth it for the views though....

Link to my ride - https://www.strava.com/activities/8901504344

Struggle event which I've entered - https://ridethestruggle.com/products/struggle-moors-hardest-sportive-in-yorkshire  

Please Like and Subscribe to the channel for more regular videos.

Follow me:
Instagram - https://www.instagram.com/cyclingunleashed/
Strava - https://www.strava.com/athletes/21407791
Facebook - https://www.facebook.com/cyclingunleashed

Credit to Videezy.com for the video overlays

videos.curation_reason: First-person ride experience specifically training for Struggle Moors event with detailed commentary on Yorkshire terrain including Boltby Bank.

videos.title: Are these idiots for real?
videos.description:
When you've watched this, Watch this - https://youtu.be/euBN2ODkFYw 
Then see if your comments are still valid!

While these signs strongly advise pedestrians to stay off cycling trails for safety, the legal situation is more complex.

The Countryside and Rights of Way Act 2000 (CROW Act) gave the public a new legal right of access to "open access land" in England and Wales. This land primarily includes mountain, moor, heath, and downland, and much of the land managed by Forestry England falls under this category. This means that, by law, the public has a right to walk on this land.

Therefore, while a sign with a crossed-out person is a critical safety warning and should be respected, it does not always represent a legal prohibition. It's a way for Forestry England to manage risk and guide people towards safer, more suitable walking routes.

This reality highlights a significant challenge for forest management: balancing public access rights with the need for user safety. 

I have my opinions on this and clearly not everyone agrees, which is absolutely fine as is your right to agree or disagree. I'm not changing my opinion on the response I got but clearly the signage is not as clear cut as it first appeared.

Let me know your thoughts in the comments below....


Follow me on Socials

videos.title: Yorkshire awaits Tour de France
videos.description: All eyes will be on northern England this summer when the Tour de France gets underway. The county of Yorkshire will be the unlikely setting for the start of one of the biggest sporting events in the world.It's become a tradition for the race to start outside France.The opening stage will see the peleton race 190 kilometres from Leeds to Harrogate.

videos.title: Extreme Hill Climbing Sportive ! 170km & 4000m Elevation Inc Porlock Toll Road, Lynmouth Ramp
videos.description:
This UK sportive takes place in the sublime Exmoor National Park - and deserves it's name, the Exmoor Epic!  It crams in 4000m of climbing into just 105miles, taking in the climb from North Moulton to the Moor, Dunkery Beacon, Porlock Toll Road, Church Hill Road, and the vertical Lynmouth Ramp.  For about 1/3 of the course we run along the stunning and unspoilt coast line, with brief respite as we tour through the mesmerizing Valley of The Rocks, from where we ascend Jose's climb - followed by a number of others, before rounding off on part of the Exmoor Forest climb on the run back home.  

It's a must do sportive for those who enjoy their climbing - and whilst typically not as steep as the Struggle Dales in Yorkshire, it includes more climbing, and felt every bit as hard!  This was indeed a Hard Hundred (...and a bit ! )

Please do like comment and subscribe !

Follow my journey  
Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/bikeracingwithoutmercy/
Strava: https://www.strava.com/athletes/BRWM

quotes.text: I think I'm also going to sign up for the struggle event that's in four weeks... I thought it was a much better idea to get out and do some miles get a decent riding get some hills in today as a bit of training for that.
This is a lot much fun, it's never a good time when you can't even stop going down it, breaks pretty much on full... Jesus, where's the road go.

key_challenges.name: 11 percent climb
key_challenges.description: The race sets out its stall early with straight into an 11 percent climb, double digits within the first half kilometer.

key_challenges.name: Dunkery Beacon
key_challenges.description: Described as the peak of Dunkery Beacon, reached at quarter distance on the climbing with 1100 meters elevation gained.

key_challenges.name: Lim Muth Ram
key_challenges.description: Described as lurking menacingly, characterized as steep terrain that makes riders want their mummy.

terrain_notes.text: There's a lot of mud and challenging conditions, especially when it rains hard and gets cold, making for miserable riding conditions.
The toll road climb is described as a hell of a lot easier, about five or six percent grade with lovely climbing characteristics.

gear_mentions.text: Arm warmers are recommended for comfort when it rains hard and gets cold during the race.
Mechanical issues can occur with rear derailleurs, where the chain comes off when trying to get into the smallest gear on steep climbs.

race_day_tips.text: Don't burn any matches until you need to - ride yourself gently into the race early on.
Avoid making rash pacts to go full gas early in the ride when you're only 35 miles in with another five hours and 65 miles ahead, plus significant climbing remaining.
Plan for pit stops to stuff jerseys full of carbohydrates during the longer climbs.

additional_quotes.text: 4000 meters of climbing much of it's steep and i can't get into the smallest gear without the chain coming off

rider_intel.search_text: The Struggle Moors presents 4000 meters of challenging climbing over 160 kilometers, with much of it steep terrain. The race starts aggressively with an 11 percent climb and double digit gradients within the first half kilometer. Key climbs include Dunkery Beacon at the quarter distance mark and the intimidating Lim Muth Ram. The course features varied terrain from difficult steep sections to easier toll road climbs of 5-6 percent grade. Weather conditions can be harsh with rain creating muddy, miserable conditions. Mechanical issues like derailleur problems are common on the steep climbs. Riders emphasize pacing strategy, avoiding burning matches early when facing five hours and 65 miles of significant climbing ahead. The challenging nature makes this a true test of endurance cycling in the North York Moors.
